Speaker: JTK
Manx: V'ad ooilley, va'd ayns, ayns, yn, v'ad baghey ayns y clieau va big creaghyn dy moain oc, well cha row ad dy bragh laccal geayl.
English: They were all, they were in, in, the, they were living in the mountain they had big stacks of peat. well they weren't ever lacking coal.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Ayns, ayns clieau, well there's moain, oh big creaghyn oh bunnys feedyn, feed y- yard l- long.
English: In, in (the) mountain, well there's peat, oh big stacks oh nearly twenties, twenty y- yard l- long.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Oh, cha row ad, v'ad ooilley, as va ram ooilley Skyll-, ooilley Skylley, Skylley Creest cur lesh yn moain voish y clieau neesht. V'eh foddey share na'n moain ayns Balley Laghey.
English: Oh, they weren't, they were all, and there were a lot all Kir-, all Kirk, Kirk Christ (Lezayre) bringing the peat from the mountain too. It was much better than the peat in Ballaugh.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Va, v'ad, yn un red v'ayn as v'eh choud v'ad tayrn eh.
English: Yes, they were, it was [?] the same/only thing and it was as far they were dragging it.

Speaker: WC
Manx: Cre'n traa ayns y vlein v'ou goll dy geddyn yn moain, ayns, ayns yn arragh?
English: What time in the year were you going to get the peat, in, in the spring?

Speaker: JTK
Manx: As va shin cur lesh thie eh lurg yn ouyr.
English: And we'd bring it home after the harvest.

Speaker: WR
Manx: As row oo giarrey lesh faayl?
English: And were you cutting with a turf spade?

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Va Caine, va'n, va'n fer nagh row dooinney ny ben v'eh shen giarrey eh.
English: Caine was, was the, the fellow who wasn't a man nor a woman he was cutting it.

Speaker: WR
Manx: Oh, va shen yn keird echey?

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Va, shen yn keir-, keeayl [sic = keird] echey giarrey yn moain.
English: That, was his pro-, sense [=profession] cutting the peat.

Speaker: WR
English: And how much were you paying him?

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Oh well, v'eh three skillin, yn laa v'eh geddyn y traa shen.
English: Oh well, it was three shillings, a day he was getting at that time.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Aye, as va'n feallee va skeayley, skillin.
English: And the folks who were spreading, a shilling.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Oh tra ren mee hoshiaght, tra ren mee fakin eh hoshiaght va, va yindys atchimagh er [sic] son va, va, va'n perrickyn er, as cooat er y red err. [laughs].
English: Oh when I first, when I saw him first I, I was terribly surprised because, he, he had petticoats on, and a coat on the thing err. [laughs]

Speaker: JTK
Manx: And did you ever hear of him?
English: And did you ever hear of him?

Speaker: JTK
Manx: You never heard of Caine the Petticoats?
English: You never heard of Caine the Petticoats?

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Cha ren, and he was neither a man nor a woman.
English: No, and he was neither a man nor a woman.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: And he was dressed in women's petticoats all, petticoats and a frock and then he had a, a coat over it, over, a little ...
English: And he was dressed in women's petticoats all, petticoats and a frock and then he had a, a coat over it, over, a little ...

Speaker: JTK
Manx: ... little jacket over it, like, and a hat and all on him, and bits of whiskers on his, sides of his cheek here.
English: ... little jacket over it, like, and a hat and all on him, and bits of whiskers on his, sides of his cheek here.

Speaker: DF
English: He was like a woman, is he?

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Va, va, v'eh goll-rish dy bee ben erbee dy row cooat er.
English: Yes, yes, he was like any woman that had a coat on.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: And I'll tell you where he was living, you'll know Gob y Vollee?
English: And I'll tell you where he was living, you'll know Gob y Vollee?

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Well, v'eh, v'eh, Gob y Vollee, well he was living over in a house and it's fell, and it's by the right-hand side of the road.
English: Well, he was, he was, Gob y Vollee, well he was living over in a house and it's fell, and it's by the right-hand side of the road.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: The Rock, the, Killip the Rock is there nearly, but then it's over Ballaugh side of that.
English: The Rock, the, Killip the Rock is there nearly, but then it's over Ballaugh side of that.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: And the ruins is still there and he was living in a thatched house there.
English: And the ruins is still there and he was living in a thatched house there.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: You'll see the ruins if ever you be going past, on your right-hand side that there past the Rock.
English: You'll see the ruins if ever you be going past, on your right-hand side that there past the Rock.

Speaker: WC
Manx: Oh ta mee er n'akin eh. She.
English: Oh I have seen it. Yes.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Yeah, nee, nee oo, nee shiu fakin eh ayns shen.
English: Yeah, you, you'll, you will see it there.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Err, nee, roish, roish nee shiu, roish nee shiu cheet dys Ballacrye.
English: Err, before, before you, before you come to Ballacrye.

Speaker: WC
English: Yes, it's a ruin there now.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Tholtagyn, tholtagyn t'ayn nish. Ta'n, ta'n, paart jeh'n thie ayn agh t'eh ooilley lhieggit, as ...
English: Ruins, ruins there now. There is, there is, some of the house there but it's all knocked down, and ...

Speaker: JTK
English: It had a garden and everything.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: As v'eh son, v'eh son, v'eh son, v'eh son buinn traagh lesh yiarn chammah dooinney erbee.
English: And he could, he could, he could, he could cut hay with a scythe as well as any man.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Va, buinn lesh yiarn, yiarn folderagh ...
English: Yes, cutting with a scythe, a scyth ...

Speaker: JTK
Manx: ... as v'eh buinn traagh as v'eh, ta, v'eh jannoo ayns y moain, shen v'eh as, v'eh giarrey eh.
English: ... and he was cutting hay and he was, yes, he was doing (it) in the peat, that he was and, he was cutting it.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Ta, well ooilley traa va enn aym er ansh erbee, but, roish shen va'n mummig marish.
English: Yes, well all the time I knew him anyway, but before that the mother was with him.

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Have you never heard of him?
English: Have you never heard of him?

Speaker: JTK
Manx: He was neither a man nor a woman, he was what they called a 'moffet head' of.
English: He was neither a man nor a woman, he was what they called a 'moffet head' of.
Notes: See moffie, mophy, morphodite < hermaphrodite in OED

Speaker: JTK
Manx: That's, that's the English of it.
English: That's, that's the English of it.

Speaker: WC
English: He was quite an odd man!

Speaker: JTK
Manx: [laughs] I've got the English of it, I haven't know what the, what Manx for to put on that.
English: [laughs] I've got the English of it, I haven't know what the, what Manx for to put on that.

Speaker: DF
English: Oh I don't know at all!

Speaker: JTK
Manx: Oh dooinney eh hene, oh lieh, lieh dooinney lieh ven.
English: Oh a man himself, oh half, half man half woman.
